  • Azure Virtual Machines, Managed Disks, Virtual Network.
  • Google Compute Engine, Persistent Disk, VPC.

When to pick IaaS:

  • You need deep control over OS and middleware.
  • You're lifting-and-shifting existing VMs from on-prem.
  • You have specialized software that requires custom OS config.
  • You want the closest cloud analog to 'renting a server rack.'

Most flexibility, most responsibility — IaaS gives you the most knobs but also puts the most work on you. PaaS and SaaS remove knobs in exchange for removing work.

hack.memory: IaaS = renting an empty apartment. Provider gives you walls, plumbing, electricity. You bring furniture, paint, food. Maximum flexibility, maximum effort.
hack.practice: Spin up an AWS EC2 instance. Notice: AWS gave you a VM but you had to pick the OS, patch it, configure SSH, install your app, set up backups. That full stack of 'you own it' is IaaS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know IaaS = you manage OS + apps; provider manages hardware + virtualization. EC2 is the canonical example.

1.2.f.info:
PaaS (Platform as a Service) rents you a fully managed application platform — OS, runtime, language stack, middleware — so you only worry about your application code and data. The provider handles everything below your app.

What the provider manages:

  • Physical hardware and facility.
  • Virtualization.
  • OS — patched and secured automatically.
  • Runtime — Node, Python, .NET, Java, etc.
  • Middleware — load balancing, auto-scaling, logging infrastructure.

What you manage:

  • Application code.
  • Application configuration (env vars, connection strings).
  • Data in databases or storage the platform provides.

Canonical examples:

  • AWS Elastic Beanstalk, AWS Fargate, AWS Lambda (serverless is often categorized as PaaS or FaaS).
  • Azure App Service, Azure Functions.
  • Google App Engine, Google Cloud Run.
  • Heroku — the classic OG PaaS.

Benefits:

  • Deploy code in minutes — push Git, platform builds and runs.
  • Auto-scaling built in.
  • No OS patching, no runtime upgrades, no infrastructure management.
  • Developer-friendly — focus on business logic, not infrastructure.

Tradeoffs:

  • Less flexibility than IaaS — stuck with the platform's supported runtimes and versions.
  • Vendor lock-in risk — moving off Elastic Beanstalk or App Engine can be painful.
  • Opaque troubleshooting — harder to debug deep infrastructure issues.

Sweet spot: web apps, APIs, event-driven workloads where developers want to ship fast without managing infrastructure.

hack.memory: PaaS = renting a furnished apartment. Walls, furniture, appliances included. You bring clothes and groceries (your app and data). Easier to move in, less control over the furniture.
hack.practice: Deploy a 'Hello World' app to Heroku or Azure App Service. You wrote 5 lines of code; platform handled OS, runtime, scaling, SSL, logs. That's PaaS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know PaaS = you manage app + data only; provider handles everything else. Elastic Beanstalk and App Service are canonical.

1.2.f.info:
SaaS (Software as a Service) is fully managed software delivered over the internet. The provider owns and operates everything — you just log in and use the app. You manage only your data and user accounts inside it.

What the provider manages: everything — facility, hardware, virtualization, OS, runtime, middleware, application code, updates, security, availability.

What you manage: your users, roles, settings inside the app, and your data.

Canonical examples (you use several every day):

  • Microsoft 365 — Outlook, Word, Excel, Teams in the browser/app.
  • Google Workspace — Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Drive.
  • Salesforce — CRM.
  • Dropbox, Box — file storage.
  • Slack, Zoom — collaboration.
  • ServiceNow, Workday — IT and HR workflows.
  • GitHub, GitLab (as a service) — code hosting.

Benefits:

  • Instant value — sign up, start using.
  • No IT infrastructure at all.
  • Automatic updates and security patches.
  • Access from anywhere.

Tradeoffs:

  • Minimal customization — you live in the vendor's product.
  • Data-sovereignty questions — your data is in the vendor's cloud.
  • Recurring cost — monthly per user fees add up for large orgs.
  • Vendor risk — if the SaaS goes down, your workflow goes down (e.g., M365 outages).

Exam framing: 'Which cloud model requires the customer to only manage their data?' → SaaS. 'Microsoft 365 is an example of which service model?' → SaaS.

hack.memory: SaaS = hotel room. Show up, use it, leave. Staff cleans, maintains, runs everything. You own only your toothbrush (your data).
hack.practice: List every SaaS you logged into this week: Gmail, Slack, Zoom, Dropbox, Jira. You manage nothing but your account and content. That's SaaS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: 100% guaranteed — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS identification question. Memorize M365 and Salesforce as SaaS examples.

1.2.f.info:
The shared responsibility model defines exactly who is responsible for what in a cloud deployment. The split depends on the service model (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS). Misunderstanding this model is how breaches happen — the classic 'but I thought AWS patched that!' story.

Provider is always responsible for:

  • Physical security of data centers.
  • Physical hardware.
  • Virtualization / hypervisor.
  • The provider's own control plane (APIs, management console security).
  • The cloud 'fabric' — core networking, storage, compute.

Customer is always responsible for:

  • Identity and access management (who can log in to what).
  • Data classification and protection (encryption keys, backups, retention).
  • Workload configuration — S3 bucket permissions, security groups, firewall rules.
  • Application code and application security.
  • Compliance with regulations that apply to the customer's industry.

The split shifts with the service model:

  • IaaS — customer responsible for OS, middleware, runtime, app, data. Provider for hardware + hypervisor.
  • PaaS — customer responsible for app + data only. Provider for everything below.
  • SaaS — customer responsible for data, user access, and app configuration. Provider for the entire software stack.

Cisco phrasing: 'Security OF the cloud' = provider. 'Security IN the cloud' = customer. This AWS-style split is the clearest mental model.

Real-world breach patterns almost always involve the customer's responsibilities: misconfigured S3 buckets, weak IAM policies, stolen API keys, unpatched customer-managed VMs. The provider hardly ever breaches — the customer does.

hack.memory: Shared responsibility = apartment lease. Landlord fixes the roof, wiring, plumbing (security OF the building). You lock your own door, don't leave windows open, don't give strangers your key (security IN the apartment). Most apartment break-ins happen because the tenant left the door unlocked.
hack.practice: For each layer — data, app, runtime, OS, hypervisor, hardware — mark who's responsible in IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Draw it as three columns. This is the most common CCNA cloud exam format.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: very commonly tested — know the split changes with service model and that customer is always responsible for data + access.

1.2.f.info:
Hybrid cloud combines on-prem infrastructure with one or more public clouds, with secure connectivity between them. Workloads run wherever they fit best, and data moves between environments as needed.

Typical patterns:

  • Cloud burst — normal load runs on-prem; when demand spikes, extra capacity spins up in the cloud.
  • Sensitive-on-prem, elastic-in-cloud — regulated data stays local; dev/test/analytics happens in the cloud.
  • DR to cloud — primary site is on-prem; disaster recovery site is a cloud region. Cheaper than a second physical DC.
  • Cloud-first, legacy on-prem — new workloads go cloud-native; older apps live out their life on-prem.

Connectivity between on-prem and cloud:

  • Site-to-site VPN over internet — cheapest, OK for low-bandwidth/infrequent use.
  • Dedicated private connection — AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, GCP Cloud Interconnect. Private circuits from on-prem to the cloud provider, higher bandwidth, lower latency, SLAs. Bypass the public internet.
  • SD-WAN with cloud on-ramps — SD-WAN tunnels extend into cloud, unifying the architecture.

Strengths: best of both models (control + elasticity), supports compliance while taking advantage of cloud scale, natural migration path from on-prem to cloud-first.

Weaknesses: operational complexity (two platforms), network latency between environments, security has to be consistent across both, data egress costs from cloud can surprise you.

hack.memory: Hybrid = owning a house + keeping a rental unit for guests. Sensitive stuff at home (on-prem). Overflow and experiments in the rental (cloud). Bridge between them = front door (VPN or private link).
hack.practice: Sketch a hybrid architecture: on-prem DC with internal apps + a cloud VPC with a scalable web tier. Draw the connection (Direct Connect + VPN backup). Label what workload runs where and why.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know hybrid = mix of on-prem + cloud connected privately; know the 3 private-link products by name.

1.2.f.info:
Direct cloud connections are dedicated, private circuits from an on-prem data center (or colocation facility) to a public cloud provider. They bypass the public internet entirely, giving predictable bandwidth, low latency, and SLA guarantees that VPN-over-internet cannot match.

The three you must know:

  • AWS Direct Connect — AWS's dedicated-link product. 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, or 100 Gbps circuits to an AWS region via a Direct Connect location (carrier hotel).
  • Azure ExpressRoute — Microsoft's equivalent. 50 Mbps to 100 Gbps. Can connect to Azure, Microsoft 365, and Dynamics.
  • Google Cloud Interconnect — Google's version. 'Dedicated Interconnect' for direct fiber or 'Partner Interconnect' via a service provider.

Why use them instead of VPN-over-internet:

  • Predictable latency and bandwidth — no internet congestion.
  • Higher throughput — multi-Gbps circuits available.
  • SLA — the provider contractually commits to availability.
  • Security — traffic stays on a private circuit, never touches the public internet.
  • Lower data-egress costs — cloud providers typically charge less for traffic over these private links than over the public internet.

Typical deployment: enterprise colocates network gear in a carrier hotel (e.g., Equinix DC) that also hosts a Direct Connect / ExpressRoute point of presence. A cross-connect in the colo links the two. Redundancy: two circuits via two different colos, for full HA.

When NOT needed: small workloads, occasional cloud use, anywhere a site-to-site IPsec VPN over the internet is good enough.

nodes: AWS Direct Connect / Azure ExpressRoute / GCP Interconnect

hack.memory: Direct Connect / ExpressRoute / Interconnect = private driveway to the cloud. Internet VPN = going through public streets. Both get you there; the private driveway is predictable and avoids traffic.
hack.practice: Memorize the three product names and their providers: AWS → Direct Connect, Azure → ExpressRoute, GCP → Cloud Interconnect. These are vocabulary exam points.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: 'AWS's dedicated private cloud link is called ___' → Direct Connect. Pure memorization.

1.3.a.info:
Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) is an optical cable with a very narrow core (8-10 microns) that allows only a single ray (mode) of light to propagate. Because there is only one path, there is no modal dispersion — the light stays coherent over very long distances.

Key specs:

  • Core diameter: 8-10 μm — roughly the width of a human hair.
  • Cladding: 125 μm (industry standard for both SMF and MMF).
  • Light source: laser (typically 1310 nm or 1550 nm wavelengths). Lasers are more expensive and more precise than the LEDs used for multimode.
  • Distance: 10 km on standard optics, up to 100+ km on long-haul optics (DWDM systems can push thousands of km with amplifiers).
  • Jacket color: yellow (industry convention).
  • Common connectors: LC (small form-factor) and SC (square push-pull).

Where you find it:

  • Long-haul WAN and metro backbone links between buildings or cities.
  • Building-to-building campus uplinks longer than ~550 m.
  • Data-center interconnects (DCI) between buildings or regions.
  • Service-provider infrastructure end-to-end.

Standards you'll see (exam-relevant):

  • 1000BASE-LX — 1 Gbps over SMF, up to ~10 km at 1310 nm.
  • 10GBASE-LR — 10 Gbps over SMF, up to 10 km.
  • 10GBASE-ER — 10 Gbps over SMF, up to 40 km.

Cost: SMF cabling is inexpensive; the optics (SFP/SFP+ lasers) are where cost lives. 10 km SMF SFP+ is typically 2-3x the price of short-reach MMF SFP+.

hack.memory: Single-mode = sniper rifle. One focused laser, tiny core, shoots across the city. Yellow jacket = gold = expensive = long range.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, connect two switches with a 1000BASE-LX link and the right SFP modules. Note the cable icon is yellow. Compare with a copper gig link — same bits per second, different physics.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 (cabling). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: memorize SMF = 8-10 μm core, yellow, laser, 10+ km. Versus MMF's orange/aqua, LED/VCSEL, shorter reach.

1.3.a.info:
Multimode Fiber (MMF) has a larger core (50 or 62.5 μm) that allows multiple rays (modes) of light to propagate simultaneously, bouncing off the cladding at different angles. The larger core lets cheaper light sources work — but it also causes modal dispersion, which limits distance.

Core sizes and OM grades:

  • OM1 — 62.5 μm core. Orange jacket. Legacy. 10G reaches ~33 m.
  • OM2 — 50 μm core. Orange jacket. Legacy. 10G reaches ~82 m.
  • OM3 — 50 μm core, laser-optimized. Aqua jacket. 10G up to ~300 m.
  • OM4 — 50 μm, higher-grade laser-optimized. Aqua (sometimes violet). 10G up to ~400 m, 40G/100G also supported at shorter distances.
  • OM5 — 50 μm, wideband. Lime green jacket. Supports SWDM for 40/100G over a single pair.

Light source: LED (older) or VCSEL (Vertical-Cavity Surface-Emitting Laser) for modern OM3/OM4/OM5. VCSELs are cheaper than the DFB lasers used in SMF.

Typical usage:

  • Inside data centers — ToR to spine, server to leaf, SAN links.
  • Between floors in a building.
  • Short backbone runs on a campus (under ~550 m).

Common standards:

  • 1000BASE-SX — 1 Gbps MMF, up to ~550 m.
  • 10GBASE-SR — 10 Gbps MMF, 300 m on OM3, 400 m on OM4.
  • 40GBASE-SR4, 100GBASE-SR4 — parallel MMF using MPO connectors.

Cost: both fiber and optics are cheaper than SMF for short runs. Trade distance for price.

hack.memory: Multimode = shotgun. Lots of light modes bouncing through a bigger tube, cheaper lasers, shorter effective range. Orange = old MMF (OM1/2). Aqua = modern MMF (OM3/4). Lime = OM5.
hack.practice: Make a 5-row table: OM1–OM5. For each, note: core size, jacket color, max 10G distance. Quiz yourself. OM3/OM4 at 10G (300/400 m) is the exam-critical pair.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: know OM1-OM5 colors and that OM3/OM4 are the common modern DC fibers for 10G/40G.

1.3.a.info:
Modal dispersion is the reason MMF has shorter reach than SMF. In a fiber with a large core, light rays (modes) enter at slightly different angles, travel slightly different path lengths through the core as they bounce off the cladding, and therefore arrive at the far end at slightly different times. As distance grows, the spread between fastest and slowest rays grows too. Eventually, one bit's pulse 'smears' into the next bit's pulse and the receiver can no longer tell them apart.

Why SMF doesn't have this problem: the tiny 8-10 μm core only admits one mode of propagation. All the light takes essentially the same path, so pulses stay sharp even after 80 km.

Consequences for network design:

  • MMF distance shrinks as speed increases. 1000BASE-SX reaches 550 m on OM2. 10GBASE-SR reaches only 300 m on OM3 (400 m on OM4). 40GBASE-SR4 drops to 100-150 m.
  • Faster standards require better fiber grade (OM3 → OM4 → OM5) to extend reach.
  • For anything over ~550 m, you have to switch to SMF.

Mitigations (why OM3/OM4 exist): laser-optimized multimode fibers refine the refractive-index profile of the core to minimize modal dispersion when paired with VCSEL light sources, pushing 10G reach from 82 m (OM2) to 300-400 m (OM3/OM4).

Exam framing: know that modal dispersion is the cause of MMF's distance limit. If you see 'why does MMF have shorter reach than SMF?' the answer references modal dispersion.

nodes: Multiple modes bounce at different angles
Receiver can't distinguish bits → distance limit

hack.memory: Modal dispersion = runners on different lanes of a curved track. Inside lane runs less distance than outside lane — they start together but arrive apart. The farther the race, the wider the gap. Same thing with light modes in a big fiber core.
hack.practice: Sketch a fiber cross-section. Draw 3 light rays entering at different angles. Inside the fiber, they bounce and take different-length paths. One arrives first, one last — that spread is modal dispersion.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: conceptual — 'modal dispersion' is the named reason MMF distance < SMF distance.

1.3.a.info:
Fiber connectors physically terminate a fiber strand and plug into transceivers or patch panels. The CCNA expects you to recognize the common types by name and appearance.

LC (Lucent Connector)

  • Small form-factor — half the size of SC.
  • Push-pull design with a latch, like RJ-45.
  • Dominant in modern SFP/SFP+/QSFP optics because two LC fit where one SC would.
  • Works with SMF and MMF.

SC (Subscriber Connector)

  • Square, push-pull design.
  • Older but still common on patch panels and older gear.
  • Works with SMF and MMF.

ST (Straight Tip)

  • Round bayonet-mount (twist to lock).
  • Older — rarely on new gear, common on legacy MMF runs.

MPO / MTP

  • Multi-fiber push-on — 8, 12, 24, or more fibers in one connector.
  • Used for 40G and 100G parallel optics (40GBASE-SR4 uses 4 lanes × 10G, 100GBASE-SR4 uses 4 lanes × 25G).
  • High-density DC cabling.

Polishing types: UPC (blue, standard), APC (green, angled — lower reflection, used for precision applications). Never mate UPC to APC — the polish angles don't match and signal suffers.

Duplex vs simplex: most data links need a pair (TX + RX), so connectors are typically installed in duplex pairs. LC and SC are commonly clipped into duplex assemblies.

hack.memory: LC = little connector (half the size). SC = square connector. ST = stick and twist (bayonet). MPO = many-port one (parallel fiber for 40G/100G).
hack.practice: Google images of LC, SC, ST, MPO. Drill visual recognition — the exam occasionally shows images and asks you to identify the connector type.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: recognize LC and SC by name at minimum. MPO shows up in modern DC contexts.

1.3.a.info:
Category 5e (Cat5e) is the minimum copper UTP standard for modern Ethernet networks. 'e' stands for 'enhanced' — it's an improved version of the old Cat5 with tighter specs for crosstalk and signal-to-noise ratio, which lets it carry gigabit Ethernet.

Specs:

  • Frequency: 100 MHz bandwidth rating.
  • Speed: 1 Gbps (1000BASE-T).
  • Max distance: 100 m (328 ft).
  • Conductors: 4 twisted pairs, 8 wires total, terminated in RJ-45 connectors.
  • Shielding: none — it's UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair).

Where it's used:

  • Access-layer desktop drops — PC to wall jack to switch.
  • Small office / home office cabling.
  • IP phone + PC drops (often with PoE).
  • VoIP and low-speed camera infrastructure.

Why it persists: cheap, widely installed, totally fine for 1G to the desk, and 1G is still the dominant access-layer speed in most enterprises.

What it can't do: 10 Gbps is not reliably supported on Cat5e. Short runs (under ~30 m) may work, but there's no standards-backed 10G over Cat5e. For 10G you need Cat6 (at 55 m) or Cat6a (at 100 m).

RJ-45 pinout: T568A and T568B are the two 8-wire pin assignments. Most US installations use T568B. Straight-through cable = same standard on both ends. Crossover = T568A on one end, T568B on the other (modern switches auto-MDIX so crossovers are rarely needed).

hack.memory: Cat5e = the default blue cable stacked in every IT closet. 1 Gbps, 100 meters, forever. Not sexy, just reliable.
hack.practice: Identify the cables at your desk or in the office — likely Cat5e or Cat6. Check the jacket printing — it says 'Cat5e' or 'CAT 6' explicitly. Note the RJ-45 ends.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: know Cat5e supports 1 Gbps at 100 m. Don't confuse Cat5 (old, 100 Mbps) with Cat5e (1 Gbps).

1.3.a.info:
Category 6 (Cat6) is the next step up from Cat5e. Tighter twists, more insulation, and sometimes a plastic spline between pairs give it better crosstalk performance. That lets it run at higher frequencies and carry more data over the same copper.

Specs:

  • Frequency: 250 MHz bandwidth rating (2.5x Cat5e).
  • Speed @ 100 m: 1 Gbps — same as Cat5e for 1000BASE-T.
  • Speed @ 55 m: 10 Gbps — Cat6 can do 10GBASE-T but only for short runs.
  • Conductors: 4 twisted pairs, RJ-45.
  • Often has a spline (plastic separator) between pairs to reduce crosstalk.

The 10G-at-55m gotcha: this is the number Cisco loves to test. Cat6 supports 10 Gbps but only up to 55 meters (164 ft) — about half the standard 100 m. For full 100 m 10G, you need Cat6a. The 55 m limit exists because alien crosstalk (interference between adjacent cables) becomes a limiting factor at 10 Gbps on Cat6.

Where it's used:

  • Premium access-layer drops in newer installations.
  • Short 10G runs in server rooms or DC patching.
  • Common in recent construction (last 10-15 years).

Cost: Cat6 is slightly more expensive than Cat5e per foot but more forgiving of future upgrades — if your building might get 10G to the desk in a few years, Cat6 or Cat6a is the future-proof choice.

hack.memory: Cat6 = Cat5e's athletic older sibling. Same 1 Gbps distance (100 m), but can sprint 10 Gbps for 55 m. The 'short-burst 10G' cable.
hack.practice: Flashcard this exact fact: 'Cat6 supports 10G at ___ meters' → 55. This is a classic exam numeric trap and the #1 Cat6 detail.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: 100% tested — know Cat6 does 1G at 100m OR 10G at 55m.

1.3.a.info:
Category 6a (Cat6a) — 'a' for 'augmented' — extends Cat6 to support full 10 Gbps at the full 100 m range. It is the baseline copper standard for any new installation that wants 10G to the endpoint.

Specs:

  • Frequency: 500 MHz (2x Cat6).
  • Speed: 10 Gbps (10GBASE-T) at the full 100 m.
  • Conductors: 4 twisted pairs, RJ-45.
  • Shielding: often has per-pair foil shielding (F/UTP or S/FTP) or is tightly jacketed to reduce alien crosstalk.
  • Thicker and stiffer than Cat6 — harder to bend tightly, takes more space in conduit.

Why it exists: Cat6's 55 m limit for 10G was a real operational pain. Cat6a was designed specifically to push 10GBASE-T to the same 100 m reach as 1 Gbps on Cat5e.

Where it's used:

  • New building cabling (the 'install once, run for 20 years' cable).
  • Data-center TOR-to-server runs where copper is cheaper than fiber.
  • Wi-Fi 6/6E access point drops — modern APs can burst well over 1 Gbps, so Cat6a uplinks are ideal.
  • PoE++ installations (802.3bt) — thicker copper handles heat better.

Cat6 vs Cat6a exam summary:

  • Cat5e: 1G @ 100 m.
  • Cat6: 1G @ 100 m OR 10G @ 55 m.
  • Cat6a: 10G @ 100 m.

Memorize this three-row table; it's on every CCNA practice exam you'll see.

hack.memory: Cat6a = the upgrade Cat6 always needed. Same 10 Gbps speed, but runs the full 100-meter marathon instead of giving up at 55.
hack.practice: Write the 3-row table from memory every morning for a week. Cat5e / Cat6 / Cat6a with their max speeds and distances. After a week it becomes automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: 100% guaranteed — Cat6a = 10G at full 100 m.

1.3.a.info:
The 100-meter (328-foot) limit on UTP copper is one of the most repeated facts in networking — and for good reason. It applies to all twisted-pair Ethernet categories (Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6a, Cat7) regardless of speed.

Why 100 m: the limit is a function of signal attenuation, crosstalk, and timing budget in the Ethernet MAC's collision window (even in full-duplex, the round-trip signal quality degrades beyond 100 m). The IEEE chose 100 m as the design target and built every UTP Ethernet standard around it.

Breakdown within the 100 m:

  • 90 m of horizontal run — from the telecom room patch panel to the wall jack at the user's desk.
  • 10 m of patch cables — 5 m from switch to patch panel plus 5 m from wall jack to device.
  • Total must stay ≤ 100 m end to end, switch port to endpoint.

What to do beyond 100 m:

  • Deploy another switch (IDF — Intermediate Distribution Facility) within the 100 m reach. Stack or uplink via fiber.
  • Switch to fiber — SMF or MMF for runs longer than 100 m.
  • Use media converters for short copper-to-fiber extensions.

Common exam trap: a question describes a 120-meter cable run and asks why the link is unstable. The answer is 'exceeds the 100 m UTP limit.' Beyond 100 m, symptoms include intermittent link, CRC errors, speed autonegotiation to 100 Mbps or lower, or complete failure.

Memorize: 100 m = 328 ft for UTP. 100 m is independent of category — Cat6a at 100 m is the limit, not 200 m.

hack.memory: 100 meters = the copper cliff. Cross it and your link falls apart. No category helps — Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6a all stop at 100 m. Past that, fiber or another switch.
hack.practice: In a building you know, estimate the distance from an IT closet to the farthest user desk. If over 90 m of horizontal, the install needs an IDF or fiber backbone.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: 100% guaranteed — '100 m max for UTP regardless of category'.

1.3.a.info:
SFPs (Small Form-factor Pluggable) are hot-swappable transceiver modules that plug into switch/router ports. They turn a generic physical port into whatever media + speed you need — SMF, MMF, copper, long reach, short reach. Without SFPs, every port would be hard-wired to one media type.

The speed ladder (memorize):

  • SFP — 1 Gbps. Supports 1000BASE-T (copper RJ-45), 1000BASE-SX (MMF), 1000BASE-LX (SMF).
  • SFP+ — 10 Gbps. Supports 10GBASE-SR (MMF 300-400 m), 10GBASE-LR (SMF 10 km), 10GBASE-ER (SMF 40 km), 10GBASE-T (copper 30 m for most SFPs).
  • SFP28 — 25 Gbps. Same form factor as SFP+, used on DC leaf-to-server links.
  • QSFP+ — 40 Gbps. Quad SFP — 4 × 10G lanes. Common in 40G backbone.
  • QSFP28 — 100 Gbps. 4 × 25G lanes. Common in modern DC spine uplinks.
  • QSFP-DD / QSFP56 — 200/400 Gbps. Newer, on higher-end switches.

Direct Attach Cable (DAC): a passive twinax copper cable with SFP/SFP+/QSFP connectors permanently attached. Used for short-reach (typically 1-7 m) in-rack connections. Cheaper than fiber+optics for in-rack use.

Active Optical Cable (AOC): fiber with permanently attached optics — same idea as DAC but longer reach (up to ~100 m on QSFP AOC).

Cisco quirk: Cisco switches traditionally only accept Cisco-branded SFPs by default. You can override with service unsupported-transceiver to use third-party optics, but this is a risk in production.

Exam framing: 'Which transceiver supports 10 Gbps?' → SFP+. 'Which transceiver supports 100 Gbps?' → QSFP28. Memorize the speed ladder.

hack.memory: SFP ladder: SFP = 1, SFP+ = 10, SFP28 = 25, QSFP+ = 40, QSFP28 = 100. 'Q' means quad (4 lanes) — 4×10 = 40 or 4×25 = 100.
hack.practice: Flashcard the ladder. Drill until you can answer 'Which SFP for 10G over SMF 10 km?' → 10GBASE-LR on SFP+ in 2 seconds.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: 100% guaranteed SFP speed-identification question. Memorize the ladder cold.

1.3.b.info:
Shared media Ethernet refers to the legacy Ethernet topologies where every device connected to the same physical medium and had to share its bandwidth. Examples include 10BASE5 (thicknet coax), 10BASE2 (thinnet coax), and 10BASE-T with a hub. All three share one collision domain: only one device can transmit at a time.

How it worked:

  • A single cable (coax) or a hub acted as the shared wire.
  • Every NIC tapped into that shared medium.
  • Before transmitting, a device had to listen (carrier sense) to confirm no one else was already talking.
  • If two devices transmitted simultaneously, a collision occurred — both signals corrupted each other.
  • Both devices sent a jam signal, backed off a random time (binary exponential backoff), and retried.
  • This protocol is CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection).

Key constraints:

  • One collision domain for the entire segment, no matter how many devices.
  • Half-duplex only — you can't simultaneously send and receive on a shared wire, because a transmitter listening for a collision would hear its own signal.
  • Effective throughput dropped fast as devices were added — typically 40-60% of raw bandwidth under load.

Why it's dead: switched Ethernet replaced it. A switch gives every port its own collision domain, enables full-duplex, and eliminates collisions entirely. Hubs disappeared from enterprise networks in the early 2000s.

Why it's still on the exam: CSMA/CD, half-duplex, and collision-domain counting questions are rooted in this model. You don't need to deploy it, but you must understand it to answer duplex and collision-domain questions.

hack.memory: Shared media = one walkie-talkie channel. Everyone takes turns. If two talk at once = collision, both shut up and retry. CSMA/CD = 'listen first, talk if quiet, shut up if someone else cuts in'.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, build a network with a HUB (not a switch) and 4 PCs. Simulate traffic from all of them simultaneously — you'll see collisions and retries. Swap the hub for a switch — collisions vanish.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: know 'shared media = hub = 1 collision domain, half-duplex, CSMA/CD'.

1.3.b.info:
A hub is a Layer 1 device that repeats any incoming electrical signal out every other port, with no intelligence. It does not learn MACs, does not switch, does not check frames. It's electrically equivalent to the old coax shared-wire topology — just wrapped in a box.

Hub behavior:

  • A frame arrives on any port.
  • The hub regenerates the signal and broadcasts it out every other port.
  • Every attached device sees every frame — they filter based on destination MAC at the NIC.
  • Only one device can transmit at a time across all ports combined.
  • Collisions span the entire hub and, in chained hubs, across all interconnected hubs.

Collision domain and broadcast domain with a hub:

  • 1 collision domain total for the hub (and every other hub daisy-chained to it).
  • 1 broadcast domain total.
  • Duplex: half-duplex only. CSMA/CD required on every connected device.

Exam-critical fact: a 12-port hub has 1 collision domain, not 12. Contrast a 12-port switch which has 12 collision domains (one per port). This comparison shows up constantly.

Why they're obsolete: switches cost the same or less, deliver full-duplex, eliminate collisions, and scale linearly with port count. Hubs are functionally inferior in every way.

Where you might still see one: a network tap (intentionally uses hub-like behavior to mirror traffic), or ancient gear in a museum-quality environment. Not in a real modern network.

children.name: Repeats signal out all other ports

hack.memory: Hub = megaphone. Anyone who talks, everyone hears, only one at a time. Dumb, loud, obsolete.
hack.practice: Count collision/broadcast domains on a 12-port hub vs a 12-port switch. Hub: 1 CD, 1 BD. Switch: 12 CD, 1 BD (assuming all ports in one VLAN). Burn this into muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: 100% guaranteed — 'a hub has how many collision domains?' → 1.

1.3.b.info:
Point-to-point Ethernet is modern switched Ethernet, where each device has a dedicated link (point-to-point) to a switch port. There is no shared medium — each port has its own physical connection between exactly two endpoints (device and switch).

Consequences of point-to-point:

  • Each switch port is its own collision domain. A 48-port switch = 48 collision domains.
  • Full-duplex is possible. Each direction (TX, RX) uses a separate wire pair, so send and receive happen simultaneously.
  • No collisions. Two devices can't collide because each link has only two endpoints and separate TX/RX pairs.
  • CSMA/CD is not needed on full-duplex links (and is disabled by default on modern NICs in full-duplex mode).
  • Throughput is 2x a half-duplex link of the same speed — 1 Gbps full-duplex = 1 Gbps each direction = 2 Gbps aggregate.

Dense micro-segmentation: every port is its own collision domain, so performance scales with port count. Adding users doesn't slow the network the way it did on a hub.

Broadcast domain behavior doesn't change. All ports in the same VLAN still share a broadcast domain — point-to-point only splits collision domains, not broadcast domains. To split broadcast domains, you still need VLANs or L3 boundaries.

How to verify full-duplex on Cisco:

show interfaces Gi0/1 | include duplex

Output should show 'Full-duplex, 1000Mb/s'. If it says 'Half-duplex' on a modern switch-to-PC link, you have a duplex mismatch — almost always caused by mixing manual and auto-negotiation settings on the two ends.

hack.memory: Point-to-point = private phone line. Two ends, both can talk at once (full-duplex), no one else on the line (no collisions). Switched Ethernet = a phone line per port.
hack.practice: On any switch, run 'show interfaces status'. Every port shows full-duplex and its own speed. That's point-to-point switching in production.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: 'How many collision domains on a 24-port switch?' → 24.

1.3.b.info:
A collision domain is a portion of the network where two devices transmitting simultaneously will collide. It's a physical-layer concept tied to how signals share a wire.

Key counting rules (memorize cold):

  • Hub: all ports share 1 collision domain. 24-port hub = 1 CD.
  • Switch: each port = 1 collision domain. 24-port switch = 24 CDs.
  • Router: each interface = 1 collision domain boundary. A router with 4 interfaces terminates 4 CDs.
  • Full-duplex link: collisions are impossible even though the link technically counts as a collision domain of size 2. Most exam contexts still count it as 1 CD per port.

Worked example — the classic exam question:

  • A 24-port switch with 3 VLANs, connected to a 2-port hub via one switch port.
  • Collision domains: 24 (one per switch port) + 2 hub ports sharing 1 CD = but the hub's CD is already counted by the switch port it connects through. So: 23 switch ports that have dedicated CDs + 1 shared CD on the hub side = 24 CDs. In strict counting, the hub ports plus the switch port feeding them all share one CD.
  • Broadcast domains: 3 (one per VLAN on the switch) — a hub doesn't add a BD, it just extends the existing one.

Why it matters for design: more collision domains = less contention = better performance. This is the entire reason switches replaced hubs — segmenting a shared CD into per-port CDs (micro-segmentation) scales throughput with port count.

Collision domain on a fiber link: fiber switch ports are still point-to-point, full-duplex, so collisions can't occur. They count as separate collision domains in exam questions just like copper switch ports.

hack.memory: Collision domain = shared walkie-talkie channel. Hub = one channel for all users. Switch = a separate private channel per user. Router = the wall between buildings.
hack.practice: Draw 4 different topologies on paper with mixes of hubs, switches, and routers. Count collision domains for each. Then count broadcast domains. Repeat until you can do a 30-second topology in your head.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 6. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 5. Exam: at least one 'count the collision domains' question. The classic hub-vs-switch contrast is the heart of it.

1.3.b.info:
CSMA/CD = Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. It is the Ethernet protocol that lets multiple devices share a half-duplex medium without central coordination. It's the heart of legacy shared-media Ethernet and a classic exam topic.

Step-by-step:

  1. Carrier sense — before transmitting, a device listens to the wire. If another signal is present, it waits.
  2. Multiple access — any device is allowed to attempt transmission when the wire is idle. No token, no permission.
  3. If two devices start at the same moment, their signals collide on the wire.
  4. Collision detection — the transmitting devices detect the collision (their own transmission is garbled).
  5. Each sends a jam signal so all stations know a collision happened.
  6. Each device picks a random backoff time using binary exponential backoff (1st collision: wait 0 or 1 slot-times; 2nd: 0-3; 3rd: 0-7; capped at 10 doublings).
  7. After backoff, the device retries from step 1.
  8. After 16 failed attempts, the device gives up and reports a transmission error.

The slot time: in 10/100 Mbps Ethernet, the slot time is 64 bytes (512 bit times). Collisions must be detected within the first 64 bytes of the frame. If a collision is detected after 64 bytes, it's a late collision — always abnormal, usually a duplex mismatch or a cable too long.

Where CSMA/CD still matters:

  • Legacy hub networks and half-duplex links.
  • Troubleshooting: if show interfaces shows collisions incrementing, the link is probably half-duplex (often a duplex mismatch).
  • Conceptual exam questions.

Where it doesn't apply: modern full-duplex switched links. The NIC operates in full-duplex mode and CSMA/CD is administratively disabled.

hack.memory: CSMA/CD = polite conversation at a party. Listen (carrier sense). Talk when quiet (multiple access). If someone else starts at the same time (collision), both stop (jam), wait a random amount (backoff), try again. Switched + full-duplex = everyone has their own phone, no polite listening needed.
hack.practice: Draw the 8-step CSMA/CD flowchart on paper. Memorize the term for each step: carrier sense, multiple access, collision detect, jam signal, backoff, retry.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: guaranteed — name the full acronym + know the steps. Slot time 64 bytes is a bonus fact often tested.

1.3.b.info:
Duplex describes whether a link can send and receive simultaneously.

Half-duplex:

  • Send OR receive, not both at the same time.
  • Used on shared media (hubs, coax) and some legacy links.
  • Requires CSMA/CD.
  • Effective throughput is less than the raw bit rate — typically 40-60% under heavy load because of collision-induced backoffs.
  • Shows up as 'Half-duplex' in show interfaces output.

Full-duplex:

  • Send AND receive simultaneously.
  • Each direction uses a dedicated wire pair (in twisted-pair Ethernet) or separate fiber strand.
  • No collisions possible.
  • CSMA/CD is disabled.
  • Effective throughput = raw bit rate in each direction (1 Gbps full-duplex = 1 Gbps TX + 1 Gbps RX).
  • Standard on every modern switch-to-endpoint and switch-to-switch link.

Duplex mismatch — the classic bug:

  • One side set to full-duplex, other side set to half-duplex (often because of manual config on one side and auto-negotiate on the other).
  • Full-duplex side transmits freely, never waits.
  • Half-duplex side sees the full-duplex side's traffic as 'collisions' when trying to transmit.
  • Symptoms: lots of collisions and late collisions on the half-duplex side, CRC errors, dropped packets, terrible TCP throughput.
  • Fix: match both sides — either both manual to the same setting, or both auto-negotiate.

Best practice: leave both ends on auto-negotiate. Avoid hard-coding duplex unless you have a specific reason.

hack.memory: Half-duplex = walkie-talkie (push-to-talk, one direction). Full-duplex = phone call (both talk at once). Duplex mismatch = walkie-talkie on one end + phone on the other = chaos.
hack.practice: On a real Cisco switch, 'show interface Gi0/1'. Note duplex and speed. Then intentionally misconfigure one side half-duplex + other side full-duplex. Watch errors climb. Fix with 'duplex auto' on both.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 5-6. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 2. Exam: duplex mismatch symptoms (especially late collisions) are a classic troubleshooting question.

1.3.b.info:
Micro-segmentation is the property that every switch port is its own collision domain. It's the defining advantage switches hold over hubs and the reason modern Ethernet scales.

Why it matters:

  • On a hub, adding users to the network reduces per-user throughput — they all share one collision domain.
  • On a switch, adding users to the network does not reduce per-user throughput — each port has its own collision domain. Micro-segmentation means performance scales linearly with port count.
  • Enables full-duplex on every port, doubling effective bandwidth compared to half-duplex hub links.
  • Eliminates collisions entirely on the switched links — collision counters should be zero.
  • Improves security (compared to a hub) — one device doesn't automatically see every other device's traffic, because frames are only forwarded to the destination's port.

Worked numbers:

  • Hub with 24 PCs sharing 100 Mbps: aggregate throughput = 100 Mbps (minus collision overhead). Per user = ~4 Mbps under contention.
  • Switch with 24 PCs each on 100 Mbps: aggregate throughput = 2400 Mbps (24 × 100). Per user = 100 Mbps full-duplex (200 Mbps aggregate per link).

Caveats:

  • Micro-segmentation splits collision domains, not broadcast domains. All ports in the same VLAN still share one broadcast domain.
  • Doesn't prevent all eavesdropping — ARP poisoning, port mirroring, or compromising the switch can still expose traffic. Micro-segmentation is a side effect, not a security feature.

Exam framing: 'What is the main performance advantage of a switch over a hub?' → micro-segmentation (per-port collision domains, full-duplex, no contention).

hack.memory: Micro-segmentation = private phone lines per desk. Hub = one shared speakerphone in the middle of the office. Switch = everyone gets their own phone line. Productivity goes up, collisions go to zero.
hack.practice: Count the difference: 24-port hub vs 24-port switch at 1 Gbps. Hub aggregate = 1 Gbps for everyone. Switch aggregate = 24 Gbps + full-duplex. The math makes the concept obvious.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 6. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 5. Exam: know micro-segmentation = one CD per port, the defining benefit of switches over hubs.
